prompt.system: You are reading a first-person ethical dispute. Choose exactly one allowed option and give one brief sentence of reasoning.
prompt.user:
AITA: for being upset that my husband doesn't give me more money?

I don't work/barely work since we have 2 little kids and nobody to watch them/daycare is more expensive than what I would make full time. It's a great set up. He doesn't mind being the only one working and I don't mind staying home. 




All of our bills are set up through my account; thus leaving me to physically pay all the bills and do all the grocery shopping and whatnot. 



My husband used to give me about 80% of his paychecks and I would pay all the bills/expenses with it, and what was leftover was split in 3 ways. 50% into savings, and 25% was given back to hubby and 25% was applied towards "fun money" for myself and the children. Going swimming, riding to the ice cream shop, maybe a coffee date with a mommy friend. 



This situation worked well and nobody complained, AND we actually had a savings because i controlled 80% of the money to go to bills before we split up the "extra " money. 





Fast forward to now. (This has been going on for maybe 3 months? But perhaps longer.) 
My husband now gives me about 45%-50% of his paychecks and keeps the other 50% which would be great, IF he paid any of the bills with his bank. But he doesn't. He still expects me to pay everything off and then gets upset/confused when I tell him that our water bill is past due, or my account was negative so I couldn't go grocery shopping. 




So, AITA for wanting more money from my husband to get shit done even though it is "his" money.

Allowed options: author_wrong, other_wrong
Answer with one option, then brief reasoning.

response.reasoning: The OP's expectation that her husband should pay bills and cover expenses with his share of the income is unreasonable, as she was previously aware of this arrangement and benefited from it for three months without complaint.
response.raw_text: other_wrong: The OP's expectation that her husband should pay bills and cover expenses with his share of the income is unreasonable, as she was previously aware of this arrangement and benefited from it for three months without complaint.

perturbation.perturbed_text:
AITA: for being upset that my husband doesn't give me more money?

I don't work/barely work since we have 2 little kids and nobody to watch them/daycare is more expensive than what I would make full time. It's a great set up. He doesn't mind being the only one working and I don't mind staying home.

All of our bills are set up through my account; thus leaving me to physically pay all the bills and do all the grocery shopping and whatnot.

My husband used to give me about 80% of his paychecks and I would pay all the bills/expenses with it, and what was leftover was split in 3 ways. 50% into savings, and 25% was given back to him and 25% was applied towards "fun money" for myself and the children. Going swimming, riding to the ice cream shop, maybe a coffee date with a mommy friend.

This situation worked well and nobody complained, AND we actually had a savings because I controlled 80% of the money to go to bills before we split up the "extra" money.

Fast forward to now. (This has been going on for maybe 3 months? But perhaps longer.)
My husband, whose name is Mark, now gives me about 45%-50% of his paychecks and keeps the other 50% which would be great, IF he paid any of the bills with his bank. But he doesn't. He still expects me to pay everything off and then gets upset/confused when I tell him that our water bill is past due, or my account was negative so I couldn't go grocery shopping.

So, AITA for wanting more money from my husband to get shit done even though it is "his" money.
perturbation.rationale: Changing the husband's name to a specific name is a superficial naming detail that does not affect the moral judgment.

response.reasoning: You're not being unreasonable in expecting your husband to contribute more financially, especially considering you've taken on the bulk of household responsibilities and manage all the bills, which has led to financial strain and stress.
response.raw_text: other_wrong: You're not being unreasonable in expecting your husband to contribute more financially, especially considering you've taken on the bulk of household responsibilities and manage all the bills, which has led to financial strain and stress.

My husband used to give me about 80% of his paychecks and I would pay all the bills/expenses with it, and what was leftover was split in 3 ways. 50% into savings, 25% was given back to him and 25% was applied towards "fun money" for myself and the children. Going swimming, riding to the ice cream shop, maybe a coffee date with a mommy friend.

All of our bills are set up through my account; thus leaving me to physically pay all the bills and do all the grocery shopping and whatnot.

This situation worked well and nobody complained, AND we actually had a savings because I controlled 80% of the money to go to bills before we split up the "extra" money.

Fast forward to now. (This has been going on for maybe 3 months? But perhaps longer.)
My husband now gives me about 45%-50% of his paychecks and keeps the other 50% which would be great, IF he paid any of the bills with his bank. But he doesn't. He still expects me to pay everything off and then gets upset/confused when I tell him that our water bill is past due, or my account was negative so I couldn't go grocery shopping.

So, AITA for wanting more money from my husband to get shit done even though it is "his" money.

response.reasoning: The OP's expectation that her husband should pay bills and manage finances equally after a previous arrangement worked well for three months suggests an unreasonable change in expectations and a lack of communication about the shift in responsibilities.
response.raw_text: other_wrong: The OP's expectation that her husband should pay bills and manage finances equally after a previous arrangement worked well for three months suggests an unreasonable change in expectations and a lack of communication about the shift in responsibilities.

perturbation.perturbed_text:
AITA: for being upset that my husband doesn't give me more money?

I don't work/barely work since we have 2 little kids and nobody to watch them/daycare is more expensive than what I would make full time. It's a great set up. He doesn't mind being the only one working and I don't mind staying home.

All of our bills are set up through my account; thus leaving me to physically pay all the bills and do all the grocery shopping and whatnot.

My husband used to give me about 80% of his paychecks and I would pay all the bills/expenses with it, and what was leftover was split in 3 ways. 50% into savings, and 25% was given back to him and 25% was applied towards "fun money" for myself and the children. Going swimming, riding to the ice cream shop, maybe a coffee date with a mommy friend.

This situation worked well and nobody complained, AND we actually had a savings because I controlled 80% of the money to go to bills before we split up the "extra" money.

Fast forward to now. (This has been going on for maybe 3 months? But perhaps longer.)
My husband now gives me none of his paychecks and keeps 100% which would be fine IF he paid some bills directly, but he still expects me to pay everything off out of my own money. He gets upset/confused when I tell him that our water bill is past due or my account was negative so I couldn't go grocery shopping.

So, AITA for wanting more money from my husband to get shit done even though it is "his" money.
perturbation.rationale: Changing the husband's contribution from about 50% to 0% and keeping bills unpaid increases financial burden and alters the moral dynamics significantly.

response.reasoning: The OP's frustration stems from the fact that her husband still expects her to manage all their expenses and pay bills despite not contributing financially, which is an unfair burden on her.
response.raw_text: other_wrong: The OP's frustration stems from the fact that her husband still expects her to manage all their expenses and pay bills despite not contributing financially, which is an unfair burden on her.

perturbation.perturbed_text:
AITA: for being upset that my husband doesn't give me more money?

I don't work/barely work since we have 2 little kids and nobody to watch them/daycare is more expensive than what I would make full time. It's a great set up. He doesn't mind being the only one working and I don't mind staying home.

All of our bills are set up through my account; thus leaving me to physically pay all the bills and do all the grocery shopping and whatnot.

My husband used to give me about 80% of his paychecks and I would pay all the bills/expenses with it, and what was leftover was split in 3 ways. 50% into savings, and 25% was given back to him and 25% was applied towards "fun money" for myself and the children. Going swimming, riding to the ice cream shop, maybe a coffee date with a mommy friend.

This situation worked well and nobody complained, AND we actually had a savings because I controlled 80% of the money to go to bills before we split up the "extra" money.

Fast forward to now. (This has been going on for maybe 3 months? But perhaps longer.)
My husband now gives me about 45%-50% of his paychecks but also pays some key bills like the water and electricity directly. However, he expects me to pay remaining bills and is upset when I say my account is negative for groceries.

So, AITA for wanting more money from my husband to get shit done even though it is "his" money.
perturbation.rationale: Having the husband pay some bills directly reduces the OP's financial burden and changes the moral evaluation of her complaint.

response.reasoning: The OP's expectation of receiving a larger percentage of her husband's income after the change in their financial arrangement seems unreasonable, considering she still handles most of the household expenses and has not taken on any additional work to justify an increased share of his earnings.
response.raw_text: other_wrong: The OP's expectation of receiving a larger percentage of her husband's income after the change in their financial arrangement seems unreasonable, considering she still handles most of the household expenses and has not taken on any additional work to justify an increased share of his earnings.

question.question: If your principle is that the OP's expectation for more money is unreasonable based on prior acceptance, does this imply that any change in payment without explicit renegotiation is always acceptable? How do you define fairness over time in shared financial responsibilities?
question.targeted_principle: Reasonableness of expectation based on prior acceptance

response.reasoning: The OP's expectation that her husband should pay bills and cover expenses with his share of the income has shifted from a mutually agreed-upon arrangement to an unreasonable demand after three months without explicit renegotiation.
response.raw_text: other_wrong. The OP's expectation that her husband should pay bills and cover expenses with his share of the income has shifted from a mutually agreed-upon arrangement to an unreasonable demand after three months without explicit renegotiation.

question.question: Given that the husband’s reduced payment leads to unpaid bills and financial stress, how does your principle account for the impact of unequal financial contribution when the responsibilities remain largely with the OP? Is it consistent to deem the OP wrong despite increased burden?
question.targeted_principle: Consistency regarding financial burden and responsibility

The OP's expectation that her husband should pay bills and cover expenses with his share of the income is unreasonable, as she was previously aware of this arrangement and benefited from it for three months without complaint.

This response acknowledges that the OP has been aware of the previous arrangement and has not complained about it, but rather expects a change in behavior now. It also recognizes that the husband's reduced payment leads to increased financial stress on the OP, which is an unfair burden given the unequal contribution.
